uma vez que o thc se liga nesse receptores que são chamados receptores endocanabinoides dentro do nosso cérebro ele vai começar a agir e a gente vai ter os seus diferentes efeitos de acordo com as tem um papel importantíssimo na nossa regulação do comportamento social das nossas emoções né Principalmente que ele faz uma inibição importante da amígdala que é uma outra região cerebral que está Hey everyone, how are you all doing? Nicolas, neuroscientist here, and today I'm going to talk about a new treatment possibility for depression, including a treatment method that is good and more effective in cases where depression is resistant to both pharmacological and therapy treatments. Today's video will be very direct and to the point. It's been a while since I 've brought content to the channel, but this study is so interesting that I stopped everything I was doing just to bring you this result. So please leave your like here and subscribe to the channel so you can receive more content like this and to help this information reach the people who really need it, okay? Well, let's go. The study I'm going to talk about today—I'll put it on the screen and also leave the link in the description so you can see it in its entirety—was published on May 15th in the prestigious journal *The Sediments of National*, and it was conducted by researchers at STAGF, specifically by Enem Williams. Okay, and this study showed that it's possible, through the combination of two techniques...  Neuroscience uses two imaging techniques, which we call FH ( functional magnetic resonance imaging), and TMS (transcranial magnetic stimulation). It's possible to create a biomarker for depression, which I'll discuss a little later, explaining why biomarkers are so important and how it's possible to treat this condition with this combination of techniques. So, what they did was take 33 patients with depression of varying severity levels. 23 of these patients underwent a protocol they called SNT (Stanford neuromodulation therapy), and the other 10 received a placebo treatment, which consisted basically of the same protocol but without the magnetic stimulation. They compared these results with information from a control group of 85 healthy people. What is this protocol? They used these two techniques, as I mentioned, FH and TMS, and then they used FM (feet-in-right) to guide the stimulation. It's a brain imaging technique they used to guide the stimulation accordingly.  With the anatomy of each patient being correct, they applied high-dose TMS stimulation, okay, of magnetic pulses that are capable of modulating brain activity and making that brain activity last for a while. These brain stimulation techniques have already been used in other contexts, both for the treatment of depression and for the treatment of conditions. Some of them are more invasive, usually in the brain, so we need to implant an electrode in a region of the brain called the subcranial nucleus of these patients. When we activate this electrode, it manages to stimulate that region in a way that alleviates the symptoms of lack of movement in the brain. For depression, we also have non- invasive methods like TMS, where the current is applied to the scalp. You don't need to open the person's head, put an electrode inside their brain and do stimulation; you can simply apply a magnetic field to the scalp. This magnetic field will generate a variation in the current passing through that neural circuit. And so we can modulate, activate or deactivate a certain region, or even modulate the way the current passes through that region. What the researchers showed was precisely a change in the way this current passes from one region of the brain to another. So, how do things happen inside the brain? We have our neural circuits; generally, information travels in a specific direction, going from region A to region B. What they observed, in fact, is an inversion in the direction of the current. Normally, in people, what happens is that we have a circuit involving two regions of the brain: the anterior insula and the anterior symbiotic cortex. What do these regions do? The anterior insula is responsible for our own perception; it takes information from the body, such as heart rate, breathing rate, information about our metabolism in general, and passes this information to the anterior symbiotic cortex, which determines how we are feeling. It makes a decision about the emotion we are experiencing. Okay, normally, the direction of this communication is that the insula perceives what is happening in the body and sends information to the symbiotic cortex. In patients with depression, including more severe depression, this direction of the current is inverted.  So, it reverses, right? We have the anterior cortex sending information, as if we've already decided how to feel and send that information to the body so that we feel that way. Okay? This aligns with what psychiatrists understand about depression as well. So, the researchers were able to identify this reversal in the direction of the current and thought that through TMS they could reverse the direction of the current, bringing it back to normal. And that's exactly what they did. What they showed, interestingly, is that the more severe the degree of depression, the greater the proportion of the signal passing from one region to another in the reverse direction. So, the greater the current in the reverse direction, the better the results from the stimulation they did with TMS. TMS stimulation, as I told you, was already something used to treat depression. What's the difference between their study and the treatment method we already had? What's new here? Generally, TMS protocols for treating depression involve one session per day over several weeks, even several months, to take effect. They developed a protocol where you do... Ten TMS sessions per day, usually for about five days, and in their case, in 5 days we already start to see the desired therapeutic results, so it's a much easier and faster treatment to apply. And of course, this doesn't happen for all patients, right? About three-quarters of the patients showed this inversion in the circuit current between the anterior insula and the anterior cortex, but even so, right? This is a large proportion of these patients. And as I said, this can actually constitute a biomarker for depression, and that's sensational because we're talking about a psychiatric disorder, and until today we didn't have biomarkers for psychiatric disorders. This is like a revolution we're experiencing in psychiatry today. I can look at a neural circuit, I look at how information travels between them, that is, if the information is going from point A to point B or from point B to point A, and I can identify, based on the proportion of the signal going from B to A, the probability of that person being favored by it.  Treatment with the SNT protocol can be used for diagnostic and prognostic purposes through treatment. So it's a very interesting revolution we're experiencing, which is why I stopped everything to talk to you about this subject. We know that depression is one of the most debilitating diseases we have today, responsible for most people leaving their jobs and becoming disabled. Depression is an extremely serious matter; it's the scourge of the century, the most prevalent psychiatric disorder in the world. And we're finding alternative treatments to medication and therapy for cases where these patients don't respond well. In these cases, this should bring relief to millions of lives in the coming years. Of course, more studies need to be done so that we can better understand the long-term therapeutic effects of this therapy. But here's a good study for you that I'd like you to share with others so that more people know about this kind of advancement that neuroscience is bringing to medicine. Okay, so that's it for today's video. I'd like you to leave your like on this video.  If you enjoyed the content, subscribe to the channel and leave your comments about the topics. What did you think of the video? Anyway, leave that feedback. A hug and see you in the next video! [Music]

se liga nestas cinco curiosidades sobre a dopamina que eu separei para vocês curiosidade número um a dopamina é um neurotransmissor que atua em várias áreas do cérebro incluindo a área que controla o movimento a motivação e o sistema de recompensa curiosidade número 2 A dopamina é liberada em resposta à atividades agradáveis como comer aquela comidinha deliciosa ou praticar atividades que nos dão prazer como fazer exercícios ou ter relações sexuais isso ajuda a promover a sensação de bem-estar e Prazer curiosidade número 3 A dopamina também desempenha um papel importante na aprendizagem e na memória ela é liberada durante a realização de tarefas que exigem concentração e esforço mental ajudando a melhorar a capacidade de aprender e se lembrar de informações Vamos agora para curiosidade de número 4 a falta de dopamina no cérebro está relacionada à doenças como um mal de Parkinson e a doença de huntington no caso do Mal de Parkinson a falta de dopamina no cérebro leva sintomas como tremores e dificuldade para controlar os movimentos tá gostando do vídeo Não deixe de deixar seu like e se inscrever no canal para que este conteúdo chegue a mais e mais pessoas por último vamos para Nossa Quinta curiosidade sobre a dopamina algumas drogas como a cocaína e a metanfetamina agem aumentando a liberação de dopamina no cérebro o que pode produzir efeitos de prazer e Euforia no entanto o uso prolongado destas pode levar a diminuição da produção natural de dopamina O que pode causar dependência e efeitos colaterais negativos Então já sabe né não faça uso destas substâncias tão perigosas que mexem com o balanço químico do seu cérebro para saber mais sobre o cérebro e como ele funciona confira as leituras recomendadas e os cursos que deixei disponíveis aqui na descrição deste vídeo Vejo você na próxima
